sentences.text: Sonia Burstyn.
S-O-N-I-A B-U-R-S-T-Y-N.
Waldman.
W-A-L-D-M-A-N.
In Poland, Stadniki.
S-T-A-D-N-I-C-K-I.
Near Ostrog and bigger town, Rovno.
Abba, A-B-B-A.
Waldman, W-A-L-D-M-A-N.
Gittle, G-I-T-T-L-E.
Truchler, T-R-U-C-H-L-E-R.
It was Mamcia, M-A-M-C-I-A.
Waldman, W-A-L-D-M-A-N.
She was married with two children.
And her married name was Charbash, C-H-A-R-B-A-S-H.
[NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH].
Was Buzik-- Jewish name Itzhok-- B-U-Z-I-K, Waldman, W-A-L-D-M-A-N.
He was married to Bronia, B-R-O-N-I-A.
They had two children.
One is Ruth, R-U-T-H, and the other one was Shoshona, S-H-O-S-H-O-N-A.
May I say that she was killed?
And--
Sibling is Gene, Gene Waldman Tyrangel, G-E-N-E W-A-L-D-M-A-N, Tyrangel, T-Y-R-A-N-G-E-L.
And next is going to be me.
[LAUGHS] And then my smaller sister, which is Mania, M-A-N-I-A and Waldman, W-A-L-D-M-A-N.
Married name is Jarkon, J-A-R-K-O-N.
She had-- has two children.
One is [NON-ENGLISH], and the other one is [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] lives in Israel, and his second name is Waldman, W-A-L-D-M-A-N.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sorry.
Jarkon.
[LAUGHS]
Jarkon.
J-A-R-K-O-N.
And his sister, [NON-ENGLISH] Jarkon, J-A-R-K-O-N.
Right.
Sammy is older than Mania, sorry.
And he's Shamay, S-H-A-M-A-Y, and Waldman, W-A-L-D-M-A-N.
He changed his name from Waldman to Yaari.
So he's-- right now his name is Yaari, Y-A-A-R-I.
Yes.
We were 12 children, and six were alive, and six died from different diseases and accidents.
Before the war.
My father is a third generation born in Stadniki.
My mother is-- was born in-- gee, I forgot the town.
She was born--
Mizoch.
Mizoch.
It's M-I-Z-O-C-H, Mizoch.
And they were living up 'til they died.
The same as my father's.
He was in the mill.
And Grandma is a housekeeper.
We did have another house in Ostrog.
The other house we had a nanny and a housekeeper.
And all four children were studying in Ostrog.
And these two women were supervising us.
[NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then on vacations and holidays, we used to come to visit our parents.
Until fourth grade.
Then I went to Ostrog.
And the school was Kazemirza, K-A-Z-E-M-I-R-Z-A, and up till the 1938 war.
The, the German-- the Russians occupied our town.
And after settling down, we went to Ostrog and continued the education in Russian and Ukraine languages.
Mostly Polish and Jewish.
Very little Jewish.
Mostly Polish.
Jewish?
Very well.
I know the language to write, read, and I studied it.
[BACKROUND NOISE]
Mainly Jewish.
But they were very well acquainted with the Polish news and politics and so on.
I would say close to 1,000.
It was four Jewish families.
Three families Waldmans, and one is another family that moved to Ostrog.
Mainly my mother lit candles.
We were not very religious but observed all the holidays and all the little things about Jewishness.
And Saturday usually were the meals, chicken soup and whatever goes with it.
And all the children were there.
But since we were studying in Ostrog, so only for the holidays, vacations, and other little intermissions from studying.
No.
No.
They used to get together in one house.
They used to call neighboring villages.
And they used to pray and do everything for the holidays.
Preparations and so on were all like a collective thing.
Yes.
A very big one.
As a matter-- one, one is-- was-- the Ostrog synagogue was known as the biggest synagogue in Wolyn.
Very beautiful and very lovely decorated.
No.
It was just in between.
Not very religious, not very--
We were like one of them.
We were like a family.
We grew up together.
We went to school together.
We played together.
We ate in each other houses and slept in each other houses.
So we were very, very friendly.
Including my parents.
They were very respectful toward us.
So did we.
We tried even-- we were allowed-- to celebrate their holidays.
So we really were very close with them.
In village-- the four years that I studied there, definitely.
It was very, very respectful.
I would say about 30,000.
Out of the 30,000, over 10,000 Jews.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we had Jewish Hebrew schools.
We had a high educated people and education centers.
And very well known also city because it was a historical city.
So we had a, a lot of historical places to visit.
Right.
Right.
Oh, yes we did.
Oh, yes.
From strangers, definitely.
In schools with strangers.
On street with strangers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A lot, a lot of incidents that happened to young people.
To the older people maybe I know less because I was too young.
But there was a lot of anti-Semitic accidents.
Like Hebrew school children would go out for an intermission and play in the, in the schoolyard.
So the non-Jewish children would come out from the other side and throw stones and, and sometimes even hurt people.
In schools there were name calling, because children really didn't fight that severely.
But always just because you were Jew, you had to be in the back.
Because you were Jew, you could not attend certain school.
There was one school, [NON-ENGLISH], and you could not possibly get into that school because you were Jewish.
It's a public school.
And other things-- little things, because we tried to live in our ghettos with the Jews, so it wasn't that bad.
In Ostrog, yes.
In Stadniki, no.
We were too little of a group.
Not exactly.
No.
Very, very limited.
Very limited.
But some of them were such geniuses that they couldn't help it but hire them.
In our Ostrog, we had gymnasium and lyceum, and even Hebrew [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] college.
But mainly we used to go to Lwow, to bigger cities to have higher yet.
Yes, he did have.
I believe he had a few years of college.
My mother did not.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The rest of the family?
Of course.
My oldest sister, she did finish, as I remember, high school and then higher.
They had teachers.
The teachers came, and there were about three children or four as a group.
He lived in Stadniki, and he taught them higher education.
As far as my brother's concerned, Mother and Father felt that a boy should know more than a girl, I guess, because he did go to some higher, like college, and it was in Russian.
I can't connect it, but it was in Russian.
So here we are.
He finished his college.
As far as going down to my older sister, Gene, she finished college-- gymnasium.
And she did go to a school during the Russian occupation for a teacher.
It was pedagogy institute.
So she finished that.
Then myself, I did go to school, and I did finish two years of college and, again, privately picked up when I came to America, USA.
I did go to business college.
It's Cambridge School of Business on 42nd Street in Manhattan.
So I did finish there this college and from then on started to work.
But coming back to my brother, he had a Jewish education.
He started in Hebrew school in Ostrog.
Went to-- during the war-- after the war, went to college in higher education in Israel.
The same thing happened to my sister.
She started with Hebrew school and went to Israel to higher education there.
Oh, they were supportive.
But they were not neurotic and crazy about it.
I mean, they knew that they are Jewish people and they have to support Jews and help them as brothers and sisters.
Yes, I did.
I did belong to, gee, got me--
Betar.
And I worked there with other groups and had been there until the war.
Oh, yes.

Starting with water, swimming, kayaking, bicycle riding, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] horseback riding, that was my love-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a little tennis and ball playing, something that we learned in school.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's about, I think, all.
[LAUGHS]
Oh, skiing and skating.
I forgot.
That was my love, too.
Participating, yeah.
I always, in school, they used to choose me, because I wasn't lazy, to be the leader in some kind of games.
Tried my best to be one of everybody because it seemed like we were well off and better off than other children, I would say, in my class, my school.
So I tried to hide it kind of.
Blend in to be one of them.
So I learned it in the early years.
Very little.
Very little because we had such a beautiful place where we lived in Stadniki that people used to come to us in vacationing.
Because we had water, we had beautiful, beautiful trees around, and the healthiest atmosphere, I would say.
The healthiest place, I think.
And we loved it.
And we used to come just for the holidays, so it wasn't enough even for us.
Yeah.
So we really didn't go except to go to Rovno to visit people or other villages or so.
But we didn't travel too much.
In terms of the professions in town, what were the jobs or the work that Jews did in your area?
Well, I was a student.
So mainly schools and up until the war and during the war, even.
It's later during the war that I had to work very hard physically.
And that's about all.
Otherwise, I did not have any profession really.
I would say [SIGHS] more by Jews.
I would say a good 80% Jewish stores.
I mean, it's my opinion.
I might be wrong in the 20 different ones.
In Ostrog?
I don't know how healthy because I was too young to understand.
The Jew still was a Jew and a dirty Jew and, and they belong in ghetto and whatever it is.
And go to Israel, and-- it, it was anti-Semitic atmosphere.
No.
In Stadniki it was our hometown.
We were one of them.
We spoke-- I'm sorry.
We spoke fluently Polish and Ukraine.
I still do.
And just because of that, I think it was a big plus for us.
Every place we went-- like we went to school-- nobody could really detect my Jewish accent.
So I was accepted faster than anyone with a Jewish accent.
So we had the plus.
And Ukrainians the same thing.
There is not a slight Jewish accent because we spoke it so much.
Mainly they had their own little houses, little outskirt.
They, they, they were mingling with the Jewish peddlers and selling.
But I would say their main [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] work was the farms and around the farms, selling everything from the farms and dealing with Jews, of course.
And the biggest flea market, I would call it, or market, was, I guess, in Ostrog.

Because people from all over-- and when I say people, mostly Ukrainian, Polish people-- non-Jewish-- used to come and bring all their goods and sell and trade and sometimes you see them in the movies, you know?
Like horses and things like that.
So very big one.
In Stadniki you say that there was a Ukrainian population and a Polish population.
Correct.
The Polish population was very small.
It was like Jewish population.
They had maybe five families Polish.
And the, the rest is Ukrainian.
They did because we had-- I failed to mention to you-- a, a Polish family that practically owned Stadniki.
I forgot to tell you, which is very important.
She was one lady, two sisters, single.
They were the ones to employ these people, give them, I guess, bread and butter.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because of her being there, they better watch out with the Polish people.
Yes.
There was young people, [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH].
And they didn't have any father.
So they had only a, a mother, and her name was [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH] or [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] or [NON-ENGLISH].
Their name [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH].
Right.
Ukrainian.
Ukranian.
He was Ukrainian.
And most of the time, he took orders from that lady, from that Polish lady that owned this.
Her name was Mogenitska.
OK.
Their name were Mogenitska's Estate.
OK.
Exactly.
The mill-- everything belonged to her, all right.
And he did not-- he rented it.
He didn't work for her.
He rented it and pay rent for the ground, I guess, I guess, the-- every-- all the buildings and including the mill.
Very.
They were in very good relationship.
Very good.
So there we didn't have any problem, either.
Well, the Russians came in into the town.
They were looking for very rich people.
So when they came to us, they are right away is how rich are we?
Well, they found out that we were not.
And if we did, then they didn't find out.
[LAUGHS] So my father still was operating the mill.
We still were living in our borders, and we all went to school.
No.
No.
From the beginning, maybe a little bit because they didn't know us.
It's the rich people.
They did get Mogenitska out of the-- her place and ruined her beautiful house with books and everything else, I remember, and sent them to Siberia.
Not with us.
No.
These [INAUDIBLE] were the only ones.
Ostrog?
A lot of people.
As a matter of fact, two of our neighbors-- actually, one neighbor-- at night they took them and took them to Siberia.
No, they were Jewish people.
Jewish people wealthy.
Very wealthy.
They lived practically next door to us.
So that I remember very well.
As far as the other people, I know that there was a lot of people that were.
And some of them willingly left Poland.
